reference.title: Formatter and Style Policy
reference.description: Guidelines for respecting and configuring code formatters and style rules. Agents must detect existing configuration files and adhere to them. If no configuration exists, sensible defaults are provided. Formatting should be automated and enforced both locally and in CI to avoid “nitpick” reviews.

section.title: Discover existing configuration
section.content: Before formatting code, check for the presence of `.editorconfig`, Prettier config files (`.prettierrc`, `prettier.config.js`), ESLint config files (`.eslintrc`, `eslint.config.mjs`), Stylelint config files, `pyproject.toml` (for Black and Ruff), `rustfmt.toml`, `clippy.toml`, `go.mod` (for gofmt settings), `pom.xml` or `build.gradle` (for Checkstyle or Spotless), and `.editorconfig` (for consistent whitespace). Do not override repository‑specific settings unless directed by an ADR.

list.item: JavaScript/TypeScript – Use Prettier for formatting and ESLint for linting. For Next.js projects, extend `eslint-config-next`. Run `npx prettier --check .` and `npx eslint .` in CI.
CSS/Sass – Use Stylelint with the GDS shared config. Complement with Prettier to format CSS and SCSS. Run `npx stylelint "**/*.scss"` in CI.

Python – Use Black for formatting and Ruff for linting and fixing. Run `black --check .`, `ruff .`, and `mypy .` in CI. If the repository uses Flake8, continue to use Flake8 unless an ADR switches to Ruff.
Rust – Use `cargo fmt --check` for formatting and `cargo clippy` for linting. Avoid allowing warnings; treat them as errors.
Go – Use `gofmt -l` to check formatting, and `go vet ./...` for linting. Tools like `staticcheck` can provide additional linting.
Java – Use Spotless or google-java-format to enforce formatting, and Checkstyle or PMD for linting. Run them via Maven or Gradle plugins.
.NET – Use `dotnet format` for formatting and built‑in analyzers or StyleCop for linting.
Shell – Use `shfmt` for formatting and `shellcheck` for linting.

section.title: Pre‑commit hooks
section.content: Use tools like Husky and lint‑staged to run formatters and linters on files staged for commit. This provides fast feedback and prevents style violations from entering the codebase.

section.title: Enforcing formatting in CI
section.content: Make formatting and linting jobs required checks in your branch protection rules. The pipeline should fail if any formatter or linter reports issues. Agents must not reformat unrelated files in the same PR; run formatters in a separate fix commit if needed.
